title: Predictive Maintenance Alert Volume Trending
app: Splunk Machine Learning Toolkit (optional), Edge Hub anomaly outputs, DSDL or custom scoring jobs
spl:
index=ot sourcetype IN ("edge_hub:anomaly","edge_hub:alert") earliest=-90d@d
| timechart span=1w count as pred_alerts
| trendline sma6(pred_alerts) as alert_trend
| eventstats avg(pred_alerts) as baseline
| eval spike=if(pred_alerts > baseline*1.5,1,0)
description: ML-driven anomaly counts should rise when equipment drifts but should fall after maintenance; tracking weekly volume validates model calibration and catches alert fatigue or broken scoring pipelines.
value: ML-driven anomaly counts should rise when equipment drifts but should fall after maintenance; tracking weekly volume validates model calibration and catches alert fatigue or broken scoring pipelines.
implementation: Tag production ML alerts distinctly from threshold rules. Deduplicate repeated scores per asset per hour if the model emits bursts. Correlate spikes with maintenance windows—expected dips after work orders. If counts go to zero suddenly, validate the scoring container and HEC path. Feed counts back to data science for precision and recall reviews quarterly.
visualization: Line chart (weekly ML alert count with trend), Bar chart (alerts by asset), Single value (alerts versus 8-week average).
knownFalsePositives: Predictive maintenance alert volume changes when models are retrained, thresholds retuned, or new assets are added.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Machine Learning Toolkit (optional), Edge Hub anomaly outputs, DSDL or custom scoring jobs.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=ot` `sourcetype IN ("edge_hub:anomaly","edge_hub:alert","edge_hub:ml")` with `severity`, `model_id`, `asset_id`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Tag production ML alerts distinctly from threshold rules. Deduplicate repeated scores per asset per hour if the model emits bursts. Correlate spikes with maintenance windows—expected dips after work orders. If counts go to zero suddenly, validate the scoring container and HEC path. Feed counts back to data science for precision and recall reviews quarterly.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ot sourcetype IN ("edge_hub:anomaly","edge_hub:alert") earliest=-90d@d
| timechart span=1w count as pred_alerts
| trendline sma6(pred_alerts) as alert_trend
| eventstats avg(pred_alerts) as baseline
| eval spike=if(pred_alerts > baseline*1.5,1,0)
```

Understanding this SPL

**Predictive Maintenance Alert Volume Trending** — ML-driven anomaly counts should rise when equipment drifts but should fall after maintenance; tracking weekly volume validates model calibration and catches alert fatigue or broken scoring pipelines.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=ot` `sourcetype IN ("edge_hub:anomaly","edge_hub:alert","edge_hub:ml")` with `severity`, `model_id`, `asset_id`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Machine Learning Toolkit (optional), Edge Hub anomaly outputs, DSDL or custom scoring jobs. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ot.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=ot, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=1w** buckets — ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.
• Pipeline stage (see **Predictive Maintenance Alert Volume Trending**): trendline sma6(pred_alerts) as alert_trend
• `eventstats` aggregates the pipeline (counts, distinct values, sums, percentiles, etc.) into fewer rows.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **spike** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.

Step 3 — Validate
Run the search over at least 30 days when possible, then compare trends with operations views, edge metrics, or source historians. Tie step changes in the trend to known campaigns, seasons, or upgrades.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (weekly ML alert count with trend), Bar chart (alerts by asset), Single value (alerts versus 8-week average).
grandmaExplanation: We trend how many predictive maintenance alerts fire so a jump or drop tells us the model, assets, or process changed in a meaningful way.
